sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms remain concentrated at pilot-stage AI use and plan lower AI investment shares than the euro area average.
bullets: Slovak firms are notably more concentrated at the pilot/experimental stage than EA peers: 46.5% of SK firms report very infrequent or experimental AI use versus 32.7% in the EA, a gap of 13.8 pp.
Moderate AI use is correspondingly lower in Slovakia (17.1%) than in the EA (31.2%), a gap of 14.1 pp, while significant use is broadly comparable (SK 5.3% vs EA 6.4%).
Slovak firms estimate that 25.6% of peers in Germany, France and Italy have already invested in AI — notably above the EA average of 19.0% and Germany's own estimate of 18.4%.
For domestic peer adoption, Slovak firms estimate 14.1%, slightly below the EA average of 15.6%, a gap of 1.5 pp.
Slovak firms expect 35.0% of peers in Germany, France and Italy to invest in AI over the next 12 months (b_g4), exceeding the EA average of 29.4% by 5.6 pp.
For domestic peer investment expectations, Slovak firms report 23.7% (a_g3), notably below the EA average of 30.5%, a gap of 6.8 pp.
Incompatibility with existing systems is the leading barrier for Slovak firms not using AI more intensively (SK 28.4% vs EA 30.1%), broadly comparable to the EA average.
Lack of AI skills is cited more frequently in the EA (31.8%) than in Slovakia (25.7%), a gap of 6.1 pp, making it a relatively less prominent barrier domestically.

Slovak AI-using firms cite reducing personnel costs more frequently than EA peers (SK 31.2% vs EA 26.0%), a gap of 5.2 pp, while improving non-core processes is the top reason in both (SK 47.9% vs EA 42.2%).

Improving core area processes is notably less cited by Slovak firms than by EA peers as a second reason (SK 35.3% vs EA 38.0%), with the EA placing greater emphasis on core-process improvement overall.
Slovak firms expect to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI over the next 12 months, below the EA average of 7.3% and Germany's 8.4%, a gap of 1.8 pp relative to the EA mean.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms remain concentrated at pilot-stage AI use and plan lower AI investment shares than the euro area average.

For domestic peer investment expectations, Slovak firms report 23.7% (a_g3), notably below the EA average of 30.5%, a gap of 6.8 pp.

Lack of AI skills is cited more frequently in the EA (31.8%) than in Slovakia (25.7%), a gap of 6.1 pp, making it a relatively less prominent barrier domestically.

Slovak AI-using firms cite reducing personnel costs more frequently than EA peers (SK 31.2% vs EA 26.0%), a gap of 5.2 pp, while improving non-core processes is the top reason in both (SK 47.9% vs EA 42.2%).

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms remain concentrated in pilot-stage AI use, trailing the euro area on intensity, investment allocation, and forward-looking adoption expectations.
bullets: Slovak firms are notably more concentrated in pilot or very infrequent AI use than EA peers: 46.5% of SK firms selected code 2 versus 32.7% in the EA, a gap of 13.8 pp.
Conversely, only 17.1% of Slovak firms reported moderate AI use compared with 31.2% in the EA, indicating that pilot-stage engagement has not yet translated into deeper integration.

Slovak firms estimated that 25.6% of similar firms in Germany, France and Italy had invested in AI by mid-2025, exceeding the EA average estimate of 19.0% by 6.6 pp — the largest cross-border perception gap observed across QB1 sub-items.
For domestic peers, Slovak firms estimated 14.1% had invested in AI, marginally below the EA average of 15.6%, a gap of 1.5 pp.

Slovak firms expected only 23.7% of domestic peers to invest in AI over the next 12 months (sub-item a_g3), compared with 30.5% in the EA — a gap of 6.8 pp, the widest domestic forward-looking shortfall in the module.
For investment expectations regarding Germany, France and Italy (sub-item b_g3), Slovak firms' estimate of 31.4% was broadly comparable to the EA average of 33.3%, a difference of 1.9 pp.
Incompatibility with existing systems was the leading first reason cited by Slovak firms for not using AI more intensively (SK 28.4% vs EA 30.1%), broadly comparable to the EA at a gap of 1.7 pp.

Lack of AI skills ranked first in the EA (31.8%) but second in Slovakia (25.7%), a difference of 6.1 pp, with Slovak firms instead placing greater relative weight on perceived irrelevance of AI for their activities (SK 31.6% vs EA 26.2% on code 1).

Among AI-using firms, improving non-core processes was the primary first reason in Slovakia (47.9% of firms) versus 42.2% in the EA, a gap of 5.7 pp, while improving core processes ranked higher as a second reason for EA firms (37.6%) than for Slovak firms (33.8%).

Slovak AI-using firms cited personnel cost reduction as a first reason more frequently (31.6%) than EA peers (26.0%), a difference of 5.6 pp, pointing to a cost-efficiency orientation in domestic AI adoption.
Slovak firms expected to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI technologies over the next 12 months, compared with 7.3% in the EA and 8.4% in Germany — lower by 1.8 pp and 2.9 pp respectively.
Both SK and EA reported a median allocation of 5%, indicating the mean gap is driven by a smaller share of high-intensity AI investors in Slovakia rather than a broad-based shortfall.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms remain predominantly at pilot-stage AI use, with investment intentions and budgets trailing the euro area average.
bullets: Slovak firms are concentrated at the pilot/experimental stage: 46.5% report very infrequent or pilot AI use, compared with 32.7% in the EA — a gap of 13.8 pp.
At the moderate-use stage, Slovakia lags notably: 17.1% of Slovak firms report moderate AI use versus 31.2% in the EA, a difference of 14.1 pp.
Slovak AI-using firms cite reducing personnel costs more frequently than EA peers (SK 31.2% vs EA 26.0%), a gap of 5.2 pp.
Improving non-core area processes is the leading adoption reason in both Slovakia (47.9%) and the EA (42.2%), with Slovakia 5.7 pp higher.
Incompatibility with existing systems is the top barrier to deeper AI adoption in Slovakia (SK 28.4% vs EA 30.1%), broadly comparable to the EA average.

Lack of AI skills is a less prominent barrier in Slovakia (25.7%) than in the EA (31.8%), a gap of 6.1 pp, while 'AI is not useful for my firm' is cited more often by Slovak firms (SK 31.6% vs EA 26.2% per chart).
Slovak firms expect to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI technologies over the next 12 months, compared with 7.3% in the EA and 8.4% in Germany, leaving Slovakia 1.8 pp below the EA mean.
Slovak firms estimate that only 14.1% of similar domestic firms had invested in AI by mid-2025, 1.5 pp below the EA average of 15.6%.

In contrast, Slovak firms attribute notably higher AI investment penetration to firms in Germany, France and Italy (SK estimate: 25.6%) than EA respondents do on average (EA estimate: 19.0%), a gap of 6.6 pp.
Slovak firms expect 23.7% of domestic peers to invest in AI over the next 12 months, 6.8 pp below the EA average of 30.5%, the largest domestic expectation gap across all questions.
For firms in Germany, France and Italy, Slovak respondents expect 31.4% to invest, broadly comparable to the EA average of 33.3% (gap of 1.9 pp), and above their estimate for domestic peers.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms are concentrated in early-stage AI adoption, with lower investment intent and a focus on non-core processes relative to the euro area.
bullets: Slovak firms are notably more concentrated in pilot or very infrequent AI use than the EA: 46.5% of SK firms fall into this category versus 32.7% in the EA, a gap of 13.8 pp.
Moderate AI use is substantially less prevalent in Slovakia (17.1%) than in the EA (31.2%), a difference of 14.1 pp, while significant use rates are broadly comparable (SK 5.4% vs EA 6.4%).
Slovak firms favour AI for improving non-core processes (47.9%) more than the EA (42.2%), while core-process improvement is cited by only 11.7% of SK firms versus 21.3% in the EA — a gap of 9.6 pp.
The 9.6 pp shortfall in core-process AI use is consistent with the lower share of Slovak firms at moderate or significant adoption stages observed in QA1.

Incompatibility with existing systems is the leading barrier in Slovakia (28.4%), whereas lack of AI skills tops the EA list (31.8%), pointing to different primary constraints on deeper adoption across the two geographies.
AI cost concerns rank notably higher among Slovak firms as a first-cited reason (31.5%) than in the EA (26.1%), a gap of 5.4 pp.
Slovak firms plan to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI over the next 12 months, below the EA average of 7.3% and Germany's 8.4%, a gap of 1.8 pp versus the EA.
The lower planned AI investment share is consistent with Slovak firms' concentration in early-stage adoption and their relatively lower intent to expand AI use.

Slovak firms estimate that 25.6% of peers in Germany, France and Italy had invested in AI by mid-2025, notably above the EA average estimate of 19.0% — a gap of 6.6 pp — suggesting Slovak firms perceive large-economy peers as more advanced adopters.
For domestic peers, Slovak firms estimated 14.1% had invested in AI by June 2025, marginally below the EA average of 15.6%, a difference of 1.5 pp.

Slovak firms expect only 23.7% of domestic peers (a_g3) to invest in AI over the next 12 months, compared with 30.5% in the EA — a gap of 6.8 pp — while their expectations for peers in Germany, France and Italy (b_g3: 31.4%) are broadly comparable to the EA (33.3%).

Under the alternative threshold (a_g4), Slovak firms' domestic peer expectation of 23.4% remains below the EA's 26.1%, while the b_g4 estimate of 35.0% exceeds the EA (29.4%) by 5.6 pp, consistent with Slovak firms viewing large-economy markets as more AI-intensive.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms are at an earlier stage of AI adoption than the euro area, with lower intensity use and lower planned AI investment shares.
bullets: Slovak firms are notably concentrated at the pilot/very infrequent use stage: 46.5% of SK firms report this level versus 32.7% in the EA, a gap of 13.8 pp.
Moderate AI use is correspondingly lower in Slovakia (17.1%) than in the EA (31.2%), a difference of 14.1 pp, confirming that SK adoption remains at an earlier stage.
Among Slovak firms using AI, improving non-core processes is the leading motivation (47.9% vs EA 42.2%), a gap of 5.7 pp.
Reducing personnel costs is cited more frequently in Slovakia (31.2%) than in the EA (26.0%), suggesting a stronger cost-efficiency orientation among Slovak AI adopters.
Incompatibility with existing systems is the top barrier in Slovakia (28.4%), broadly comparable to the EA (30.1%), a gap of only 1.7 pp.

Distrust of AI-based results ranks second in Slovakia (19.8%), while in the EA it is lack of AI skills (31.8%) — a gap of 12.0 pp versus SK's 25.5% on lack of skills — pointing to a different barrier profile.
Slovak firms expect to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI over the next 12 months, compared with 7.3% in the EA and 8.4% in Germany, a gap of 1.8 pp versus the EA.
Both regions share a median of 5%, indicating that the mean difference is driven by a smaller share of high-AI-investment firms in Slovakia rather than a broad-based shortfall.

Slovak firms estimate that 25.6% of similar firms in Germany, France and Italy had invested in AI by June 2025, notably above the EA mean of 19.0% and the German self-estimate of 18.4%, a gap of 6.6 pp versus the EA.
For domestic peers, Slovak firms estimate only 14.1% had invested in AI, below the EA mean of 15.6%, a modest gap of 1.5 pp.
For expected AI investment among similar domestic firms over the next 12 months, Slovakia's mean of 23.7% is below the EA's 30.5%, a gap of 6.8 pp.
For Germany, France and Italy, Slovak firms' expectations (35.0%) are broadly comparable to the EA (33.3%) and above Germany (28.4%), with all medians at or near 30%.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms cluster in early-stage AI adoption, with lower intensity use, investment allocation, and forward expectations than the euro area average.
bullets: Slovak firms are notably concentrated at the pilot/very infrequent use stage: 46.5% of SK firms report this level versus 32.7% in the EA, a gap of 13.8 pp.
Conversely, only 17.1% of SK firms report moderate AI use compared with 31.2% in the EA, a difference of 14.1 pp, pointing to a shallower intensity distribution in Slovakia.
Slovak AI-using firms more often cite reducing personnel costs as a primary reason (31.2% SK vs 26.0% EA), whereas EA firms more frequently report improving core processes (37.6% EA vs 33.8% SK).
Improving non-core processes is the leading stated reason in Slovakia (47.9%), exceeding the EA share of 42.2% by 5.7 pp, consistent with a cost- and efficiency-oriented adoption pattern.
The most common barrier cited in Slovakia is 'AI is not useful for my firm' (31.7% SK vs 26.1% EA), a gap of 5.6 pp that is the largest single difference across barrier categories.
Incompatibility with existing systems is the second most cited barrier in Slovakia at 28.4%, broadly comparable to the EA's 30.1% (gap of 1.7 pp).
Slovak firms expect to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI over the next 12 months, notably below the EA mean of 7.3% and Germany's 8.4%, a gap of 1.8 pp versus the EA.
The chart values (SK 5.5%, EA 7.3%) differ from the text description (SK 7.2%, EA 9.1%); the analysis above uses chart-reported figures as the primary source.

Slovak firms estimate that 25.6% of similar firms in Germany, France and Italy had invested in AI by mid-2025, notably above the EA average of 19.0% and Germany's self-reported 18.4%, a gap of 6.6 pp versus EA.
For domestic peers, SK firms estimate 14.1% had invested, modestly below the EA average of 15.6% and Germany's 17.3%, a gap of 1.5 pp versus EA.
Slovak firms expect only 23.7% of domestic peers to invest in AI over the next 12 months, notably below the EA average of 30.5% (a gap of 6.8 pp), consistent with lower domestic AI momentum.

For expected investment by firms in Germany, France and Italy, SK respondents estimate 35.0%, above the EA average of 29.4% and Germany's 28.4%, a gap of 5.6 pp versus EA — mirroring the pattern seen in QB1.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms remain concentrated at pilot-stage AI use and allocate less investment to AI than the EA average.
bullets: Slovak firms are notably more concentrated at the pilot/experimental stage than EA peers: 46.5% of SK firms report very infrequent or pilot use versus 32.7% in the EA, a gap of 13.8 pp.
Moderate AI use is substantially less prevalent in Slovakia (17.1%) than in the EA (31.2%), a difference of 14.1 pp, with significant use also below EA levels.

Slovak AI-using firms prioritise improving non-core area processes (47.9%) as their primary reason, while EA counterparts most frequently cite improving core area processes (37.6%), with SK at only 12.0% on that code.
The 25.8 pp gap between SK (47.9%) and EA (21.3%) on improving non-core processes is the largest cross-country divergence observed across AI use reasons.

Incompatibility with existing systems is the leading barrier in Slovakia (28.4%), whereas lack of AI skills tops the EA list (31.8%), pointing to differing structural constraints across the two geographies.
Slovakia's rate on the incompatibility barrier (28.4%) is broadly comparable to the EA (30.1%), a gap of only 1.7 pp, while the skills deficit barrier is more pronounced in the EA.
Slovak firms expect to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI over the next 12 months, 1.8 pp below the EA mean of 7.3% and 2.9 pp below Germany's 8.4%, the lowest of the three shown.

Slovak firms perceive AI adoption among large-economy peers (Germany, France and Italy) to be notably higher than the EA average: SK respondents estimate 25.6% of such peers have invested in AI versus the EA estimate of 19.0%, a gap of 6.6 pp.
For domestic peer adoption, Slovak firms estimate 14.1% had invested by mid-2025, slightly below the EA mean of 15.6% (1.5 pp gap).

Slovak firms expect 35.0% of large-firm peers in Germany, France and Italy to invest in AI over the next 12 months, 5.6 pp above the EA expectation of 29.4%, while expectations for domestic large-firm peers (23.4%) trail the EA (26.1%) by 2.7 pp.
For domestic SME peers, Slovak firms expect 23.7% to invest, compared with 30.5% in the EA, a gap of 6.8 pp, the widest domestic-peer divergence across the QB2 sub-items.

sections_summary.finding: Slovak firms trail the euro area in AI intensity and planned investment, yet overestimate peer adoption in larger economies.
bullets: 46.5% of Slovak firms report very infrequent or pilot use of AI, 13.8 pp above the EA's 32.7%, while moderate use stands at 17.1% versus 31.2% in the EA.
The gap in moderate use (17.1% SK vs 31.2% EA) points to a concentration of Slovak AI engagement at the earliest adoption stage rather than scaled deployment.
Slovak AI-using firms are less likely than EA peers to cite improving core area processes as a primary reason (11.7% vs 21.3%), consistent with their lower rate of moderate or intensive AI use.
Improving non-core area processes is the top reason in Slovakia (47.9% vs EA 42.2%), reinforcing the pattern of peripheral rather than operational AI integration.

Incompatibility with existing systems is the leading barrier to greater AI use in Slovakia (28.4%), versus lack of AI skills in the euro area (31.8%); the gap on incompatibility between SK and EA is narrow (28.4% vs 30.1%).
Slovak firms cite 'AI is not useful for my firm' at 31.5% versus EA's 26.1%, the largest cross-country divergence among the barriers shown, reflecting lower perceived relevance.
Slovak firms expect to allocate 5.5% of total investment to AI over the next 12 months, below the EA's 7.3% and Germany's 8.4%, consistent with their earlier-stage adoption profile.

Slovak firms estimate that 25.6% of similar firms in Germany, France and Italy had invested in AI by June 2025, versus an EA average estimate of 19.0% — a 6.6 pp gap that implies Slovak firms hold a more optimistic view of western-economy AI penetration than EA respondents overall.
For domestic peers, Slovak firms estimate 14.1% had invested in AI by mid-2025, 1.5 pp below the EA average of 15.6%, broadly in line with the EA.

Slovak firms expect only 23.7% of domestic SME peers to invest in AI over the next 12 months, 6.8 pp below the EA's 30.5%, while expectations for large domestic peers (23.4% vs EA 26.1%) show a smaller gap.

For peers in Germany, France and Italy, Slovak firms' expectations are close to or above the EA average — 31.4% vs EA 33.3% for SMEs and 35.0% vs EA 29.4% for large firms — continuing the pattern of more optimistic assessments of western-economy AI investment.
